secarb__001_a.text: Professional Medically Qualified Background Specialty (if Medically Qualified) Dermatology Do you have a GMC Yes number? GMC number 7552607 Please supply a valid GMC number Please describe your research career to date I aspire to be an academic dermatologist, and have successfully followed a clinical academic training path to date. The NIHR Doctoral Research Fellowship (DRF) is a natural next step on my journey, enabling me to hone in on my particular interest in developing personalised management strategies for inflammatory skin disease. This will prepare me well for subsequent postdoctoral training through an NIHR Clinical Lectureship and for my eventual goal of spearheading cutting-edge research in this area as an independent Clinician Scientist. Career Background I am a Dermatology Registrar in the Thames Valley Deanery. I studied Medicine at the University of Cambridge, 2nd completing an intercalated degree in Neuroscience in 2014 (First Class Honours, ranked in cohort), and graduating with Distinction in 2017. During this period, I won 3 university and 5 college prizes for academic excellence. Following graduation, I completed a highly competitive Academic Foundation Programme (AFP) and

secarb__001_b.text: an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) in Dermatology. I have a proven track-record of successfully balancing research with clinical training, having authored 14 peer-reviewed publications with 8 as first author (h- index 9). I am well-equipped for this DRF due to my extensive research experience and strong organisational skills. Research Experience During my AFP, I extracted data and assessed the risk of bias for two systematic reviews for the Cochrane Tobacco Addiction Group, following a 4-day Training Programme organised by Cochrane UK. I also worked with Professor Laura Coates, an academic rheumatologist, to design and run a multicentre study comparing different patient-reported outcome measures in psoriatic arthritis. During this, I completed Good Clinical Practice training, secured ethical approval, wrote the study documents, coordinated patient recruitment and analysed the data. I presented my findings at the 2019 British Psoriatic Arthritis Consortium, winning the runner-up abstract award, with two subsequent first author publications in The Journal of Rheumatology. In parallel, I worked on a multicentre cohort study examining the association between immune-related toxicity and survival in metastatic melanoma patients treated with checkpoint immunotherapy.

secarb__001_c.text: Supervised by Professor Benjamin Fairfax, an academic oncologist, I led the project from conception to completion and became proficient in using R for descriptive statistics and survival analysis. My work resulted in a joint first author publication in the British Journal of Cancer. Separately, I characterised the cutaneous toxicities in this cohort, which led to an oral presentation at the 2020 Annual Meeting of the British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) and a first author publication in Clinical and Experimental Dermatology. As part of my subsequent ACF in Dermatology, I completed a PGDip in Health Research with Distinction at the University of Oxford. This provided in-depth training on study design, statistics, diagnostic and screening studies, systematic reviews, and statistical computing using R and Stata. For my 9-month research rotation, I secured £25,000 from Cancer Research UK. This funded single-cell RNA sequencing experiments to investigate gene expression changes in melanoma patients who develop immune-related toxicities following checkpoint immunotherapy. secarb__001_d.text: complement my self-directed learning. My work contributed to a co-author publication in Nature Medicine and I won two sought-after travel scholarships to present my research (European Congress of Rheumatology, World Congress of Dermatology). Since completing my ACF, I have immersed myself in full-time dermatology training while actively contributing to research. I am a peer reviewer for the journals Skin Health and Disease and the British Journal of Dermatology and have recently been awarded a prestigious part-time fellowship from the UK Dermatology Clinical Trials Network (receiving the joint-highest score), where I will receive further training in patient-focused clinical research. My diverse research experiences to date have helped me realise that my true passion lies in clinical research with a direct impact on patient care. Being halfway through specialist training, I have experienced many different subspecialties and identified inflammatory skin disease as an area combining my academic and clinical interests. I became a trainee representative for the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR), a national registry for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. In addition, I have also recently joined the BAD Biologic Therapy for Psoriasis Guideline Development Group as a trainee representative.

Teaching Experience I have taught medical students in classroom, ward and outpatient settings. I also designed and implemented a well-received junior doctor-led teaching programme for medical students during my AFP. Additionally, I have completed a Developing Teaching and Learning for Medical Educators course, and have supervised junior colleagues for multiple audits and quality improvement projects. Leadership and Management Experience During my AFP, I sat on the Steering Committee of a national conference for junior doctors and medical students to showcase their research. Within my department, I chaired weekly clinicopathological correlation meetings over a 6-month period, and I am currently responsible for organising monthly regional complex cases meetings. Public Engagement Experience I recently organised a PPI advisory event, collecting valuable feedback on my project proposal. I hope to utilise this fellowship to build further experience in public engagement and involvement, supported by the extensive expertise within our group. secpesor__001_a.text: Background Psoriasis is a common skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It causes red scaly patches on the skin and there is currently no known cure. For people with severe psoriasis, tablet or injection treatments are often used to help them get it under control. Newer injectable treatments called ‘biologics’ work better than older treatments and have helped many more people get their psoriasis under control initially. Unfortunately, not everyone can keep it under control in the long- term, even if they stay on treatment. Currently, we cannot predict if a person will keep their psoriasis under control when they stay on a particular treatment. For people with psoriasis, not knowing how long a particular treatment will work for can be extremely worrying. It also means that everyone on treatment must attend regular hospital check-ups, even if they do not actually need them. Limited NHS resources are therefore being used inefficiently, and people who need appointments may struggle to get one. Aim To create a tool to identify people who are more or less likely to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a tablet or injection treatment. This could help to personalise their long-term care and improve their treatment

secpesor__001_b.text: experience. Methods First, I will review existing research to see how common it is for people to keep their psoriasis under control on tablet or injection treatments. I will also look for individual factors, such as age and weight, which could make this more or less likely. Using two large datasets from the UK and Europe, I will then build computer models to predict how likely it is for someone to keep their psoriasis under control. This prediction will be based on routinely collected clinical data, such as age, ethnicity and smoking history. I will also look at whether including genetic data (i. e. information stored in DNA) improves how well the models perform. Finally, I will host separate group discussions with healthcare professionals (including doctors and nurses) and people with psoriasis. They will provide input on which models would be best suited for use as a tool. They will also help to make the tool more user-friendly. After creating the tool, I will invite healthcare professionals to test it, and further improvements will be made based on their feedback. secpesor__001_c.text: families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more efficiently. Dissemination I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with patients and the public to develop easily understood summaries which will be circulated widely through charities, newsletters and social media.

secsa__001_a.text: Background Psoriasis is a common, incurable inflammatory skin disease associated with high psychosocial morbidity and economic impact. Systemic therapy is often required for individuals with moderate-to-severe psoriasis to achieve disease control (i. e. clear or almost clear skin). Systemic therapy encompasses conventional systemic immunosuppressive therapy, as well as a growing list of newer, highly effective biologics. While the effectiveness of biologics has led to a rapidly expanding cohort who achieve disease control, there still exists no means of predicting whether this will persist longer-term at the individual level. Uncertainty over the longevity of disease control not only causes significant anxiety for people with psoriasis, but also results in the continuation of a one-size-fits-all healthcare model of regular follow-ups regardless of clinical need. This leads to poorer treatment experiences, inefficient resource allocation, and a worsening supply and demand mismatch in the NHS. Aims and Objectives This project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy. To do this, I will:  Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the

secsa__001_b.text: factors associated with this.  Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using real-world datasets.  Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input. Methods and Timelines Phase 1 (Months 0-9): Conduct a systematic review to determine the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time, and the clinical and genetic factors associated with this. Phase 2 (Months 7-24): Develop and validate predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using two large-scale real-world datasets from the UK and Europe to ensure generalisability. I will also explore whether including genetic data improves model performance. Phase 3 (Months 19-36): Refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool. Patient and clinician focus groups will determine clinically acceptable levels of model performance, influence the definition of risk categories, and guide user interface design. The tool will be beta tested via a clinician workshop. PPI feedback has been integral to developing this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout, including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.

secsa__001_c.text: They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact. Dissemination My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social media platforms to maximise public engagement.

secdrp__001_a.text: 1. Problem Chronic plaque psoriasis (‘psoriasis’) is an immune-mediated inflammatory disease (IMID) affecting 2% of the UK population1. It presents as red scaly skin plaques and carries high psychosocial morbidity, with a quality-of-life impact comparable to cancer and cardiovascular disease2. Psoriasis also generates economic drag, with associated absenteeism and presenteeism costing c. £1. 07 billion/year3. As psoriasis is a lifelong condition with no known cure, the goal of treatment is to achieve disease control (i. e. skin4) clear/almost clear and sustain this long-term. Individuals with moderate-to-severe disease are typically treated with conventional systemic therapy, escalating to biologics if needed5. Currently, 12 biologics are NICE- approved for psoriasis5. Although expensive (£3,000-10,000/patient/year6), they are highly effective. Individuals treated with adalimumab (TNF inhibitor) are twice as likely to achieve disease control compared to methotrexate, a first-line conventional systemic7. secdrp__001_b.text: disease control at 4 months compared to 60% with adalimumab8. Due to the success of biologics, more individuals with psoriasis are achieving disease control than ever before. However, the extent to which disease control can be sustained long-term varies, with no reliable way of predicting this currently. This uncertainty can cause significant anxiety9 and leads to a one-size-fits-all healthcare model of regular follow-ups regardless of clinical need. This results in poorer treatment experiences, inefficient resource allocation, and compounds the supply and demand mismatch in the NHS, making it harder for those in need to access timely care6,10. This project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy. Aligning with the NIHR strategic theme of Health Data Science, it will help personalise long-term management strategies. For example:  Individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility, and may be suitable for remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up (PIFU) pathways with less disruption to daily life. This will also save costs and release system capacity10.  Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies such as methotrexate, which may improve

secdrp__001_f.text: 1. Stakeholders have highlighted improved risk stratification as a key research priority This project seeks to improve stratification of individuals who have already achieved disease control based on their likelihood of sustaining it long-term. This aligns with 3 of the top 10 priorities in the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership involving patients, carers and clinicians13:  ‘What factors predict how well psoriasis responds to a treatment? ’  ‘Why do psoriasis treatments stop working well? ’  ‘What is the best way to treat sudden flare ups of psoriasis? ’ People with active psoriasis often feel stigmatised and less confident due to its visual nature14. A global survey of >8,000 reported that clear/almost clear skin empowers individuals to undertake activities including swimming and wearing more varied clothing14, highlighting the importance of sustaining disease control on wellbeing. Participants from a PPI meeting I conducted were also strongly supportive of this research. One individual stated, ‘Even though my skin has cleared on treatment, I am constantly worried about whether this will last and would like more certainty. ’ This underscores the potential for risk stratification to reduce anxiety.

secdrp__001_h.text: 2. Risk stratification tools can personalise the patient pathway, save costs and inform future research Risk stratification is commonly used in routine care, as exemplified in the management of cancer15 and cardiovascular disease16. The QRISK score is one of the best-known examples of a risk stratification tool and is NICE-recommended to guide statin initiation decisions16. Use of QRISK2 is associated with a higher proportion of statin initiations being consistent with NICE guidelines (85% vs 44% when not used17) and leads to substantial years18). cost savings (c. £918 million within 2 Risk stratification tools have recently been developed for IMIDs, for example to guide blood monitoring frequency during long-term methotrexate19 and thiopurine20 treatment. Despite psoriasis being one of the most common IMIDs, no tools currently exist to predict the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy. My project will address this gap, with potential to:  Boost patient/clinician satisfaction by reducing uncertainty.  Empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors.  Personalise long-term management strategies, aligning with the NHS Long Term Plan21.

secdrp__001_l.text: 1. How is disease control defined in psoriasis? The NICE psoriasis guidelines define disease control as a Physician’s Global Assessment (PGA) of clear/almost clear skin5. This correlates to a Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) ≤2 or a PASI 90 response (i. e. 90% baseline)4, psoriasis5. improvement from where PASI is the gold standard disease severity measure in Achievement of disease control is typically evaluated 2-6 months post-initiation of systemic therapy22. Whether this is sustained long-term (i. e. ≥6 months) is a separate, important issue, which my project will focus on. Loss of disease control (i. e. relapse) is defined as a ≥3 increase in PASI, with a minimum PASI of 523.

secdrp__001_n.text: 2. How feasible is sustained disease control on systemic therapy? The Cochrane living systematic review comparing different systemic therapies only focused on randomised controlled trial (RCT) data ≤6 months post-initiation22. However, open-label extensions (OLE) to RCTs have shed light on the proportion sustaining longer-term disease control. After starting ixekizumab (IL-17 inhibitor), 75% and 73% of individuals exhibited disease control at 3 and 13 months24. In individuals who achieve disease control on risankizumab (IL-23 inhibitor) at 7 months post-initiation, 87% and 81% maintained this at 12 and 24 months25. Comparisons of longer-term RCT data are hindered by heterogeneity of reporting timepoints and trial design. Additionally, the demographics and complexity of trial populations differ from those seen in clinical practice26. To date, only one small-scale North American real-world registry study (n=687) has been published, indicating that a lower proportion of individuals sustain disease control on biologics outside of trial settings (73%/60%/50% at 6/12/18 months respectively)27.

secdrp__001_o.text: Although real-world drug persistence studies do not directly address the proportion sustaining disease control, they provide invaluable insight into longer-term effectiveness and safety. Data from the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR) demonstrates that 46% of individuals remain on methotrexate at 12 months28, considerably lower than for those treated with biologics (75%-88% depending on the drug29). This highlights that drug choice may impact the likelihood of sustaining disease control. Despite multiple studies looking at the proportion of individuals sustaining long-term psoriasis control, their clinical utility remains limited due to there being no comprehensive summary synthesising the key findings. My project will address this through a systematic review.

secdrp__001_q.text: 3. What clinical factors are associated with sustained disease control? The rapidly growing cohort achieving initial psoriasis control is a recent phenomenon. As such, researching clinical factors associated with sustained disease control has only become a priority of late, with few studies to date addressing this directly. These include:  A North American real-world registry study (n=687)27, which identified skin itch (visual analogue scale, VAS ≥30/100) or skin pain (VAS ≥45/100) to be associated with increased relapse risk on biologics (HR=1. 47; 95% CI 1. 06-2. 03). secdrp__001_r.text: 89-0. 99) to be associated with lower odds of sustaining disease control. In contrast, higher week 4 guselkumab plasma concentrations were associated with increased odds (OR=1. 43, 95% CI 1. 07-1. 90). However, both studies are limited by small sample sizes, wide confidence intervals and a lack of validation in independent datasets. Further insight can be gleaned from studies investigating different, but related, outcomes in psoriasis. A meta- analysis of factors predicting biologic persistence31 identified female sex (HR=1. 22; 95% CI 1. 07-1. 38) and obesity (HR=1. 21, 95% CI 1. 10-1. 32) to be associated with treatment discontinuation, while concomitant psoriatic arthritis was associated with a decreased likelihood of this (HR=0. 83, 95% CI 0. 80-0. 86). secdrp__001_t.text: 32. 4% in the adherent cohort). Data from our NIHR-funded self-report platform34 (n=555) suggest that patients perceive stress, low mood and infections to be major reasons for worsening psoriasis. Additionally, factors associated with sustained disease control across other IMIDs (which share many of the same systemic therapies) include smoking and joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis35, shorter disease duration and high baseline CRP in psoriatic arthritis36, and early treatment response in lupus37. Further research is required to establish whether these factors are also relevant in psoriasis. Collectively, the literature suggests that risk stratification is possible for my target cohort. By incorporating both the direct and indirect research above, and applying their findings to large real-world datasets, I aim to build a clinically relevant risk stratification tool for routine care.

secdrp__001_x.text: 62 non-HLA single nucleotide polymorphisms associates with severe disease in the UK Biobank (OR=1. 23, 95% CI 1. 14-1. 32), where 878 individuals with severe psoriasis were compared with 5,901 individuals with non-severe psoriasis40. While evidence from other diseases (e. g. schizophrenia41) indicates that PRS can be associated with treatment response, whether this is true for psoriasis remains unexplored. Although routine genetic testing is not yet commonplace in the psoriasis population, this could soon change given the rapid expansion of genomic profiling technologies. My project will leverage recent progress in this field to determine whether combining genetic and clinical data can improve model performance in predicting the likelihood of sustained disease control.

secdrp__001_z.text: 5. Why is this research needed now? Biologics have led to a paradigm shift in psoriasis treatment, with their effectiveness rapidly expanding the cohort achieving disease control. However, ongoing uncertainty over individual likelihoods of sustaining disease control has prevented their full benefits from being realised in real-world settings. Anxiety over potential relapse remains an issue, contributing to the persistence of legacy one-size-fits-all healthcare models designed for an era reliant on less effective treatments requiring more intensive monitoring. My proposed research could deliver a step change in longer-term psoriasis management, improving treatment satisfaction and service efficiency. With the fast-growing adoption of targeted therapies for other IMIDs42, my findings may also have broader impact beyond psoriasis.

secdrp__001_{.text: 4. Aims and objectives My overarching aim is to develop a risk stratification tool to help tailor management for individuals with psoriasis who have achieved disease control on systemic therapy, thereby improving long-term outcomes. To do this, I will:  Conduct a systematic review to evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the factors associated with this (Phase 1).  Model the likelihood of sustaining disease control on systemic therapy using large-scale real- world datasets (Phase 2).  Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input (Phase 3).

Phase 1: Systematic review (0-9 months) Aim Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals who sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time, and the factors associated with this. Research questions  5. 1a – What proportion of individuals sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time?  5. 1b – What clinical/genetic factors are associated with sustained psoriasis control on systemic therapy? Design I will conduct a systematic review in accordance with PRISMA guidelines43. A comprehensive search for primary studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO. I will leverage our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group44, through which I will access a wealth of resources which could contribute to this review. These include:  Completed data extraction for 167 RCTs with 12-month efficacy data, as part of a previous project evaluating study design heterogeneity45.  Ongoing data extraction from RCTs and observational studies for a systematic review of serious adverse

secdrp__001_.text: 2. Phase 2: Model development (7-24 months) Aim Develop predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using real-world data. Research questions  5. 2a – What proportion of individuals sustain disease control over time in real-world settings?  5. 2b – How well can we predict the likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy? Datasets In preparation for this fellowship, I have secured access to two large-scale real-world datasets. BADBIR is a UK and Ireland prospective observational cohort study of people with psoriasis who have commenced systemic therapy within the previous 6 months49. Participants are recruited from >160 hospitals, with >18,000 people registered and >110,000 follow-ups to date. Comprehensive sociodemographic, clinical, treatment and disease severity data are collected at 6-monthly intervals for 3 years, then annually thereafter. Within BADBIR, >6,000 individuals have been genetically profiled via the Biomarkers and Stratification To Optimise outcomes in Psoriasis study (BSTOP), led by my supervisor Professor Smith50. The detailed phenotyping makes the BADBIR data ideal as a discovery and internal validation dataset for exploring RQ5.

The HIPPOCRATES Prospective Observational Study (HPOS) is a cohort study across 12 European countries, part of a wider Innovative Medicines Initiative-funded EU consortium addressing unmet needs in psoriatic disease51. It focuses on adults with self-reported psoriasis, without a pre-existing diagnosis of psoriatic arthritis, and is led by my supervisor, Professor Coates. Since opening in summer 2023, >1,300 people have enrolled, with a target of 25,000 by 2025. Participants self-enter sociodemographic, clinical, treatment and disease severity data every 6 months for 3 years. The self-reported data, overlap with the BADBIR data fields, and large sample size across multiple countries, make HPOS ideally suited for externally validating BADBIR findings. Inclusion criteria Adults ≥18 years with psoriasis who achieve disease control (i. e. PGA of clear/almost clear, PASI/self-assessed PASI ≤2 or PASI 90 response) post-initiation of systemic therapy, with ≥1 subsequent follow-up. Where multiple measures are available (e. g.

Preliminary filtering has identified c. 9,500 individuals from the BADBIR dataset with PASI ≤2, which is feasible for predictive model development (see sample size considerations). As HPOS is still in the early stages of recruitment, estimating the eventual number meeting the inclusion criteria is challenging. If progress is insufficient, I will apply for access to a German national psoriasis registry. This has been used to validate a previous BADBIR study53, with similar assessments performed at baseline and follow-up. Using BADBIR data only, I will repeat this analysis stratifying by drug class (methotrexate, TNF inhibitor, IL-12/IL-23 inhibitor, IL-17 inhibitor, IL-23 inhibitor). Both unadjusted and adjusted estimates will be reported. Unadjusted estimates from the Kaplan-Meier analysis will enable comparison with previous studies27,30, and adjusted estimates will evaluate the impact of key sociodemographic variables (age/sex/ethnicity/location) on the unadjusted estimates. Adjusted estimates will be based on discrete-time cloglog survival curves, to determine the proportion sustaining disease control at 6-monthly intervals for up to 3 years. To account for repeated follow-ups within individuals, a random effect will be used. Missing baseline clinical data will be accounted for by using multiple imputation methods, with estimates compared to a complete case sensitivity analysis. Missing outcome data due to missed follow-ups will be handled implicitly, under the assumption that data is missing at random. Additional sensitivity analyses will evaluate potential missing-not-at-random drop out mechanisms. RQ5. 2b. secdrp__001_.text: opinion (leveraging my supervisors' clinical experience/networks), and PPI input, accounting for the extent of missing data. Clinical variables will include sociodemographic, treatment, disease severity/impact and comorbidities (including mental health). To account for differences in the variables available within the BADBIR and HPOS datasets, three separate approaches will be used:  Approach A will use clinical variables common to both datasets. This will result in a simplified predictive model which is both internally and externally validated. This has potential to be used across different healthcare and resource settings, as only widely available variables will be included.  Approach B will use clinical variables in the more comprehensive BADBIR dataset. This will result in a more complex predictive model which will be internally validated. This has the advantage of evaluating a broader range of variables compared to Approach A.  Approach C will focus on the subset of individuals in BADBIR with clinical and genetic data, to explore whether addition of genetic data improves clinical predictive model performance. This study will be reported according to the TRIPOD guidelines55. Approach A Using clinical variables common to both BADBIR and HPOS, a penalised mixed-effects multivariable logistic

Missing data will be dealt with as in RQ5. 2a. Penalisation will use elastic net and lasso approaches to select the minimum subset of variables. I will also explore whether additional machine learning approaches (e. g. support vector machine, random forest, extreme gradient boosting) will improve model performance further. Methods including nested cross validation will be used for internal validation and to prevent overfitting. The HPOS dataset will be used for external validation. Model performance will be assessed using measures of discrimination (C-statistic) and calibration (calibration slope, calibration-in-the-large). Approach B Using all clinical variables within the BADBIR dataset, I will fit, internally validate, and assess model performance using the methods outlined above. This analysis will also be repeated, stratifying by drug class, to evaluate model stability across different treatments. secdrp__001_.text: 3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will

secdrp__001_.text: 5 hours each, consist of 6-8 participants, and be co-facilitated by a trained member of my Patient and Lay Advisory Group. A topic guide developed with PPI input will be used, with detailed probing followed by general questioning to achieve convergence to group norms58. Stakeholders will be shown the Phase 2 models, and their perspectives will inform:  Acceptable levels of model performance for clinical use.  The feasibility and acceptability of including specific variables (e. g. genetic data).  How individuals with different risk profiles are categorised (e. g. number of groups/cutoff points).  The tool’s user interface and output presentation, with reference to examples provided.  Implications for clinical practice and future postdoctoral work. To enhance end-user generalisability, I will conduct ≥3 focus groups for healthcare professionals recruited from across the UK. The groups will be divided based on background and experience to minimise power imbalance. I will also conduct ≥3 focus groups for people with psoriasis recruited from the Psoriasis Association and dermatology clinics. I will leverage our skin research inclusion toolkit (manuscript in preparation) to ensure

secdrp__001_.text: representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with disabilities. All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped into potential themes using an iterative process59. I will involve two PPI collaborators in the data analysis to minimise bias. These focus groups will establish stakeholder perspectives on required parameters for a clinically acceptable risk stratification tool. These perspectives are critical for maximising the clinical utility of risk stratification in practice and will provide generalisable insights beyond this project. Beta testing Informed by focus group discussions, I will refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool, taking the form of an online interactive dashboard (built using R Shiny). This will be trialled in a 2-hour beta testing workshop involving 20 multidisciplinary healthcare professionals. This sample size is pragmatic, based on recruitment feasibility, and will provide sufficient data to determine tool usability for future work. The primary outcome will be the proportion who successfully input data to obtain an output, and the secondary outcome will be the data input

secdrp__001_.text: 1. Dissemination Patients/Public I will work with PPI collaborators to produce Plain English Summaries and infographics, which will be shared through the BADBIR newsletter and international consortia with well-established patient networks (HPOS/BIOMAP). Through our group’s collaboration with the Psoriasis Association, my findings will also be disseminated via webinars and social media. Clinicians/Researchers My findings will be presented at national and international conferences, published in high-impact journals, and shared via the St John’s DermAcademy platform. I will also use my supervisors’ networks to reach other disciplines (e. g. rheumatology) where this work may be relevant. Policymakers My results will inform UK guideline updates via my publications and supervisors. Professor Smith chaired the NICE psoriasis guidelines committee. She also leads the British Association of Dermatologists systemic therapy guidelines committee where Professor Coates and Dr Mahil are both members.

secdrp__001_.text: 2. Outputs and impact Better risk stratification could yield significant benefits across stakeholder groups. Patients Reduce anxiety and empower individuals to change modifiable risk factors, improving satisfaction and long-term outcomes. Clinicians Facilitate earlier intervention for patients at increased risk of relapse, and provide more autonomy and flexibility for those at lower risk. Researchers Inform participant selection for future trials, e. g. those exploring dose minimisation strategies for cohorts likely to sustain disease control. Commissioners Contribute to more efficient service design, e. g. by identifying those most suitable for remote monitoring or PIFU pathways, so resources can be prioritised for individuals with the highest need.

secdrp__001_¨.text: 2 – Inclusion criteria). Failure to recruit sufficient participants for qualitative work To encourage participation, virtual attendance (with a phone-in option) will be offered at convenient times. Recruiting 24 individuals with psoriasis is feasible, based on PPI feedback, the experience of my supervisors, and our group’s established links with the Psoriasis Association. Recruiting 44 healthcare professionals may be more challenging. Allowing the same individuals to take part in the focus groups and beta testing reduces the number required to 24. I will leverage my supervisors’ national networks, including their links to the UK Dermatology Clinical Trials Network (>1,000 healthcare professionals), to maximise reach. Model performance falls below clinically acceptable thresholds As outlined in Section 3, existing evidence supports the feasibility of risk stratification in my target cohort. With multiple predictive models being developed utilising a range of methods, the likelihood of at least one being acceptable is increased. In the unlikely event that none are acceptable, the focus groups will still offer invaluable insight into patient and clinician perspectives on the required parameters for a clinically acceptable risk stratification tool. These perspectives are generalisable beyond this project and key to informing the direction of future research to

secppi__001_a.text: Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this proposal I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13 involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified. My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised. Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age, ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and systemic therapies for psoriasis. All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they emphasised that:  A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people on systemic therapy.  Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.  Personalised follow-up strategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket approach. Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the

secppi__001_b.text: likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which have shaped my proposal:  Disease control maintained over the longer term, i. e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1 and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.  Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will compare models including and excluding genetic data.  To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user interface design and beta testing. Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpful feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary. Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in the proposed research, including any training and support provided

secppi__001_c.text: PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of views, backgrounds and experiences. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options. PPI collaborators will:  Provide input on variable selection for model development in Phase 2.  Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.  Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.  Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the

secppi__001_d.text: public. Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to co-present at events. PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualitative data. I have budgeted for PPI, including participant remuneration and expenses, in line with NIHR guidance57. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI activities61. If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the public in your proposed research, please justify why

sectdars__001_a.text: Proposed training and development programme I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an academic dermatologist. Systematic Reviews I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a 5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020. I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL), which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R. In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group. Statistics and Computer Programming I have developed a strong practical grounding in statistics over the past 6 years, with experience in summarising

sectdars__001_b.text: data using descriptive statistics and conducting survival analyses (see research background). I am also familiar with the RStudio environment and have significant prior experience in using R for data wrangling, visualisation and statistical tests. This has prepared me well for this fellowship, as R will be the main programming language used for data analysis. I completed a 5-day Statistical Computing with R and Stata course in 2023 (Oxford University), which provided an introduction to statistical principles relevant to this project, including dealing with missing data, power calculations, regression modelling, and cross validation. While this gave me a basic understanding of different statistical techniques, I would benefit from further formal training during this fellowship. I will attend a 5-day course on Practical Statistics for Epidemiology using R (Oxford University) in my first year to gain experience in interpreting and presenting statistical results, and learn techniques to address bias and confounding. I will also complete a 2-day Regressions with R course (UCL) to learn how to fit different regression models, including logistic regression which is central to my analyses. To prepare for the predictive modelling component of my proposal, I will attend a 3-day Statistical Methods for Risk Prediction and Prognostic

sectdars__001_c.text: Models course (Birmingham University). This will improve my knowledge of statistical methods for developing and validating predictive models. Additionally, I will receive dedicated hands-on training from Dr Niamh Dooley, a statistician at KCL, and Dr Sam Norton, who will supervise the statistical components of my project. Genetic Epidemiology In Phase 2, I will explore whether the predictive performance of clinical models can be meaningfully improved by incorporating genetic data. To better understand different genetic data types and the construction and use of polygenic risk scores, I will attend a 5-day Genetic Epidemiology course (Bristol University). I will also benefit from a collaboration with Professor Michael Simpson at the Department of Genetics in KCL, who has significant expertise in this area. Qualitative Research Methods During my third year, I will conduct focus groups to collate stakeholder perspectives on the models developed. In preparation, I will complete two courses in my second year: a 1-day Introduction to doing Qualitative Interviews course followed by a 5-day Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods course (both Oxford University). These will yield vital knowledge on how to successfully conduct focus groups, and give me practice with coding

sectdars__001_d.text: and thematic analysis. I will build on this by completing a part-time NVivo Core Skills Online Course (NVivo Academy). Dashboard Development I plan to present the risk stratification tool as an interactive R Shiny dashboard. In my third year, I will complete the Publishing Visualisations in R with Shiny and flexdashboard course (Johns Hopkins University via Coursera). I will build on this by attending a 2-day Interactive Dashboards & Web Apps using R & Shiny course (Royal Statistical Society). I will also leverage expertise available within our group, where a project is currently underway to build an R Shiny dashboard to present pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models of biologics. PPI/E Prior to starting the fellowship, I will have completed Public Involvement Training organised by the Patient Experience Research Centre at Imperial College London. During the first year of my fellowship, I will attend the Online PPI training for researchers run by the NIHR Cambridge Biomedical Research Centre PPI team. I am particularly interested in the sessions on building and maintaining a PPI group, using PPI to help communicate research findings, evaluating PPI and planning inclusive PPI.

sectdars__001_e.text: In addition, I will build on my skills in public communication by attending a 1-day Public Engagement course (Science Museum). Our group has well-established links with the Psoriasis Association and a local inflammatory skin disease PPI network. I will benefit from this extensive expertise in involving patient partners in research. Leadership In my first year, I will complete a 6-day Emerging Clinical Leaders course (The King’s Fund). This will provide valuable leadership training which will maximise the success of this project. In my third year, I will attend a 3-day Future Leaders Academy (European Society for Dermatological Research), focusing on delegation, conflict resolution, and influencing strategic change. This presents a unique opportunity to connect with future leaders in dermatology and receive guidance from experienced mentors, greatly benefitting my postdoctoral career. Research Environment My supervisors have considerable experience supporting PhD students to completion and will provide expertise on key methodological elements of this proposal. I will be hosted by the St John’s Institute of Dermatology, a world- renowned centre of excellence in skin research with an outstanding track record of supporting clinical academic PhD fellows. The KCL Doctoral Development Programme offers a comprehensive suite of training opportunities,

RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1 Name Dr Satveer Mahil Institution St John’s Institute of Dermatology, King’s College London (KCL) Position Consultant Dermatologist and Reader Description of support provided Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at KCL. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC- funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. Dr Mahil’s translational research focuses on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies for inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She has a strong interest in optimising the management of individuals with psoriasis who have sustained disease control.

Dr Norton has considerable experience in the development of risk prediction tools and is currently supervising an NIHR DRF developing an infection prediction tool for people with immune mediated inflammatory diseases treated with targeted therapies. He has close collaborations with Dr Mahil, Professor Smith and Professor Coates, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. I have worked closely with Dr Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal. Throughout the fellowship, he will directly supervise my statistical training to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. Dr Norton has supervised 21 doctoral students, of which 12 have completed and 9 are ongoing. RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3 Name Professor Catherine Smith Institution St John’s Institute of Dermatology, King’s College London (KCL) Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Professor of Position Dermatology and Therapeutics Description of support provided Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at KCL. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience ranging from biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She sits on the Steering Committees of the key datasets for this fellowship: the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR), the Biomarkers and Stratification To Optimise outcomes in Psoriasis study (BSTOP), and the HIPPOCRATES Prospective Observational Study (HPOS). RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 4 Name Professor Laura Coates Institution University of Oxford Position Associate Professor and Honorary Consultant Rheumatologist Description of support provided Professor Laura Coates is an Associate Professor and Honorary Consultant Rheumatologist at the University of Oxford. She has supported and mentored me throughout my clinical and academic training, starting from my time as an Academic Foundation Programme doctor. We have established an excellent working relationship as a result. Professor Coates is an international expert in psoriatic arthritis, with extensive experience in early diagnosis, outcome measure development, clinical trial design and systematic review. sectdars__001_m.text: Arthritis Consortium (BritPACT). She is also a member of the British Association of Dermatologists psoriasis guideline committee. Professor Coates has an excellent track record in engaging PPI collaborators in her research, and will provide guidance on PPI activities throughout this fellowship. In addition, she is the Chief Investigator for HPOS, which provides the validation dataset for my project. Professor Coates currently supervises 5 PhD students (including 1 NIHR DRF), 3 pre-doctoral fellows and 1 international post-doctoral fellow. Her supervisees include rheumatologists, a dermatologist, physiotherapist, lawyer and statistician. She also mentors two PIs nationally. During this fellowship, she will provide essential guidance in examining the subset of individuals with concurrent psoriatic arthritis. I will leverage her broad network of connections throughout rheumatology to ensure widespread dissemination of my findings to allied medical disciplines. I will aim to meet Professor Coates monthly, with increased contact depending on the phase of the project and my training needs. sectdars__001_p.text: 1 – Design of the detailed research plan. Professor Michael Simpson Professor Michael Simpson is the Head of the Department of Medical and Molecular Genetics at King’s College London. His research focuses on understanding the molecular and cellular mechanisms through which genetic variation influences susceptibility to skin disease and treatment response. Professor Simpson is a close collaborator with our group in ongoing work aimed at examining the genetic determinants of treatment response. He will be able to provide support with the analysis outlined in Section 5.

sectdars__001_q.text: 2 – Data analysis, which explores whether the predictive performance of clinical models can be meaningfully improved by incorporating genetic data. Host Organisation Support Statement The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of the form. Statement of support R. e. Dr Weiyu Ye – Head of Department Supporting Statement My name is John McGrath, and I am the Academic Head of St John’s Institute of Dermatology within King’s College London (Guy’s Hospital campus). It is a pleasure to be asked to write a supporting statement for Weiyu Ye’s NIHR Doctoral Fellowship Application. I confirm that St John’s Institute of Dermatology, part of King’s College London (KCL), supports this application and that, if funded, the research and training will be supported and administered by KCL. I confirm that Weiyu Ye will undertake this work and is eligible, for the duration of the award, to live and work in the UK. sectdars__001_r.text: published by NIHR to support her clinical academic training and I confirm that KCL is committed to these principles and obligations. The St John’s Institute of Dermatology (School of Basic and Medical Biosciences, KCL) is an internationally renowned centre for the delivery of innovative clinical care for patients with skin diseases, cutting-edge education, training, and research. A recent independent RAND assessment ranked St John’s as the leading clinical and research institution for dermatology in the UK. St John’s occupies 1,200 square metres of state-of-the-art laboratory space (Guy’s Hospital campus) and is fully integrated into the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine's School of Basic & Medical Biosciences. An active programme of graduate education is pursued including research training (PhD and MD) and a world-renowned taught course (MSc) in Clinical Dermatology. The Institute excels in research training, with a >95% success record of its Integrated Academic Training Pathway Fellows obtaining external funding from MRC/NIHR. In addition, St John's Institute of Dermatology plays a leading role in the training of dermatologists, both in the UK and around the world.

sectdars__001_s.text: Research at St John’s targets 4 key areas: inflammatory and autoimmune skin disorders (the key area of this research proposal), cutaneous oncology, genetic skin disorders, and skin-environment interactions. Our research is supported by grants from a range of sources including NIHR, MRC, The Wellcome Trust, EU, US Foundations, British Skin Foundation, national charities, and commercial sources. Our department research income averages >£3m per year with a School average research grant income of >£25m per year. The latest submission to the REF confirmed Clinical Medicine was independently rated to have 90% of its research as world leading or internationally excellent (3 or 4*) and 84 % of impact as world leading (4*). King’s College London is part of King’s Health Partners, one of the UK’s five Academic Health Sciences Centres, where world-class research, teaching and clinical practice are brought together for the benefit of patients. The three NHS partners; Guy’s and St Thomas’, King’s College Hospital (KCH) and South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trusts, are among the highest rated Trusts in the country and compete on the world

sectdars__001_t.text: stage and attract the best clinical academics to pioneer world-leading advances in health and well-being. Dr Weiyu Ye will have full support from the extensive infrastructure within the Department, School, KCL, and King’s Health Partners. We will ensure the equipment and facilities successfully allow Weiyu to pursue her research. We will also provide extensive personal support and mentorship to allow Weiyu to fully develop her research ideas, and also to embed her career as a future leading clinical academic in dermatology. As a host organization, KCL is committed to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all, championing equality, diversity and inclusion, and research integrity. KCL has been a member of the Athena SWAN Charter since 2007 and in 2021 it received a Silver Award. In 2018, the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine was recognised with a Faculty Athena SWAN Silver award as recognition of our ongoing commitment to achieving gender equality and an inclusive environment. Dr Ye’s project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy. This has the potential to facilitate more personalised long-term management for individuals who

sectdars__001_x.text: 3. Optimise the predictive models for use as a risk stratification tool, with patient and clinician input. This work is integrated with the clinical academic activities of two Faculty members within the department, Professor Catherine Smith and Dr Satveer Mahil, who will provide direct supervision, training and mentoring. In short, I am delighted to offer this Head of Department letter of support, both for Weiyu as an excellent individual embarking on her clinical-academic training and for the impressive nature of the scientific proposal I have discussed with her and her supervisors. We offer full institutional support for this application. Please do not hesitate to contact me if any further information is required. Yours sincerely, Professor John McGrath MD FRCP FMedSci

implications: Applicant CV: Degrees and clinical/academic positions demonstrate relevant research experience and formal training.
Applicant Research Background: Extensive research experience across rheumatology, oncology, and dermatology with relevant techniques (single-cell seq, survival analysis) and formal training.
Applicant CV: Research grants, multiple publications, and numerous prizes/awards indicate impactful research outputs and recognitions.
Applicant Research Background: Strong publication record (14 papers, 8 first author, h-index 9), prizes, and £25k grant indicate impactful outputs.
Applicant CV: Lead applicant on a research grant and multiple awards suggest trajectory towards research leadership.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates leadership by leading projects, supervising juniors, chairing meetings, and sitting on national committee.
Training & Development and Research Support: Participation in Emerging Clinical Leaders and Future Leaders Academy indicates commitment to research leadership.
Applicant CV: Clinical academic roles (Academic Clinical Fellow, Academic Foundation Year) demonstrate commitment to practitioner-academic career.

Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

evidence.text: Professional Medically Qualified Background Specialty (if Medically Qualified) Dermatology Do you have a GMC Yes number? GMC number 7552607 Please supply a valid GMC number Please describe your research career to date I aspire to be an academic dermatologist, and have successfully followed a clinical academic training path to date. The NIHR Doctoral Research Fellowship (DRF) is a natural next step on my journey, enabling me to hone in on my particular interest in developing personalised management strategies for inflammatory skin disease. This will prepare me well for subsequent postdoctoral training through an NIHR Clinical Lectureship and for my eventual goal of spearheading cutting-edge research in this area as an independent Clinician Scientist. Career Background I am a Dermatology Registrar in the Thames Valley Deanery. I studied Medicine at the University of Cambridge, 2nd completing an intercalated degree in Neuroscience in 2014 (First Class Honours, ranked in cohort), and graduating with Distinction in 2017. During this period, I won 3 university and 5 college prizes for academic excellence. Following graduation, I completed a highly competitive Academic Foundation Programme (AFP) and

ambitious project which could transform long-term management for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. At this pivotal stage in my training, taking an extended period away from clinical work will allow me to concentrate fully on my growth as a researcher. I am confident that I will be able to maximise this opportunity, due to my robust training plan, my expert supervisory team, and the dynamic research environment at KCL. Completing this fellowship will position me well for a Clinical Lectureship, and bring me one step closer to my goal of becoming an impactful Clinician Scientist. Have you already No registered for a PhD? Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body? No Contextual Factors Please use this question to detail any contextual factors you wish to make the Selection Committee aware of. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:  Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.  Reduced time spent undertaking research due to a disability or caring responsibilities. This could include any physical or mental difficulty that may have impacted your research career.

Proposed training and development programme I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an academic dermatologist. Systematic Reviews I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a 5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020. I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL), which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R. In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group. Statistics and Computer Programming I have developed a strong practical grounding in statistics over the past 6 years, with experience in summarising

evidence.text: In addition, I will build on my skills in public communication by attending a 1-day Public Engagement course (Science Museum). Our group has well-established links with the Psoriasis Association and a local inflammatory skin disease PPI network. I will benefit from this extensive expertise in involving patient partners in research. Leadership In my first year, I will complete a 6-day Emerging Clinical Leaders course (The King’s Fund). This will provide valuable leadership training which will maximise the success of this project. In my third year, I will attend a 3-day Future Leaders Academy (European Society for Dermatological Research), focusing on delegation, conflict resolution, and influencing strategic change. This presents a unique opportunity to connect with future leaders in dermatology and receive guidance from experienced mentors, greatly benefitting my postdoctoral career. Research Environment My supervisors have considerable experience supporting PhD students to completion and will provide expertise on key methodological elements of this proposal. I will be hosted by the St John’s Institute of Dermatology, a world- renowned centre of excellence in skin research with an outstanding track record of supporting clinical academic PhD fellows. What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

Specialty Registrar in 08/2022 - HEE Thames Valley 0 Dermatology NIHR Academic Clinical 08/2019 07/2022 HEE Thames Valley 25 Fellow in Dermatology Academic Foundation Year 08/2018 07/2019 HEE Thames Valley 33

an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) in Dermatology. I have a proven track-record of successfully balancing research with clinical training, having authored 14 peer-reviewed publications with 8 as first author (h- index 9). I am well-equipped for this DRF due to my extensive research experience and strong organisational skills. Research Experience During my AFP, I extracted data and assessed the risk of bias for two systematic reviews for the Cochrane Tobacco Addiction Group, following a 4-day Training Programme organised by Cochrane UK. I also worked with Professor Laura Coates, an academic rheumatologist, to design and run a multicentre study comparing different patient-reported outcome measures in psoriatic arthritis. During this, I completed Good Clinical Practice training, secured ethical approval, wrote the study documents, coordinated patient recruitment and analysed the data. I presented my findings at the 2019 British Psoriatic Arthritis Consortium, winning the runner-up abstract award, with two subsequent first author publications in The Journal of Rheumatology. In parallel, I worked on a multicentre cohort study examining the association between immune-related toxicity and survival in metastatic melanoma patients treated with checkpoint immunotherapy.

Supervised by Professor Benjamin Fairfax, an academic oncologist, I led the project from conception to completion and became proficient in using R for descriptive statistics and survival analysis. My work resulted in a joint first author publication in the British Journal of Cancer. Separately, I characterised the cutaneous toxicities in this cohort, which led to an oral presentation at the 2020 Annual Meeting of the British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) and a first author publication in Clinical and Experimental Dermatology. As part of my subsequent ACF in Dermatology, I completed a PGDip in Health Research with Distinction at the University of Oxford. This provided in-depth training on study design, statistics, diagnostic and screening studies, systematic reviews, and statistical computing using R and Stata. For my 9-month research rotation, I secured £25,000 from Cancer Research UK. This funded single-cell RNA sequencing experiments to investigate gene expression changes in melanoma patients who develop immune-related toxicities following checkpoint immunotherapy. In addition to in-house training on peripheral mononuclear cell extraction, immune cell subset isolation and single-cell sequencing, I attended courses on Linux for Genomics, RNA-seq data analysis and R to

complement my self-directed learning. My work contributed to a co-author publication in Nature Medicine and I won two sought-after travel scholarships to present my research (European Congress of Rheumatology, World Congress of Dermatology). Since completing my ACF, I have immersed myself in full-time dermatology training while actively contributing to research. I am a peer reviewer for the journals Skin Health and Disease and the British Journal of Dermatology and have recently been awarded a prestigious part-time fellowship from the UK Dermatology Clinical Trials Network (receiving the joint-highest score), where I will receive further training in patient-focused clinical research. My diverse research experiences to date have helped me realise that my true passion lies in clinical research with a direct impact on patient care. Being halfway through specialist training, I have experienced many different subspecialties and identified inflammatory skin disease as an area combining my academic and clinical interests. I became a trainee representative for the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR), a national registry for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. In addition, I have also recently joined the BAD Biologic Therapy for Psoriasis Guideline Development Group as a trainee representative.

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: Strong publication record (14 papers, 8 first author, h-index 9), multiple awards/prizes, and a £25k grant indicate impactful outputs and recognition.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.
sub_criteria.rationale: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.

Professional Medically Qualified Background Specialty (if Medically Qualified) Dermatology Do you have a GMC Yes number? GMC number 7552607 Please supply a valid GMC number Please describe your research career to date I aspire to be an academic dermatologist, and have successfully followed a clinical academic training path to date. The NIHR Doctoral Research Fellowship (DRF) is a natural next step on my journey, enabling me to hone in on my particular interest in developing personalised management strategies for inflammatory skin disease. This will prepare me well for subsequent postdoctoral training through an NIHR Clinical Lectureship and for my eventual goal of spearheading cutting-edge research in this area as an independent Clinician Scientist. Career Background I am a Dermatology Registrar in the Thames Valley Deanery. I studied Medicine at the University of Cambridge, 2nd completing an intercalated degree in Neuroscience in 2014 (First Class Honours, ranked in cohort), and graduating with Distinction in 2017. During this period, I won 3 university and 5 college prizes for academic excellence. Following graduation, I completed a highly competitive Academic Foundation Programme (AFP) and

an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) in Dermatology. I have a proven track-record of successfully balancing research with clinical training, having authored 14 peer-reviewed publications with 8 as first author (h- index 9). I am well-equipped for this DRF due to my extensive research experience and strong organisational skills. Research Experience During my AFP, I extracted data and assessed the risk of bias for two systematic reviews for the Cochrane Tobacco Addiction Group, following a 4-day Training Programme organised by Cochrane UK. I also worked with Professor Laura Coates, an academic rheumatologist, to design and run a multicentre study comparing different patient-reported outcome measures in psoriatic arthritis. During this, I completed Good Clinical Practice training, secured ethical approval, wrote the study documents, coordinated patient recruitment and analysed the data. I presented my findings at the 2019 British Psoriatic Arthritis Consortium, winning the runner-up abstract award, with two subsequent first author publications in The Journal of Rheumatology. In parallel, I worked on a multicentre cohort study examining the association between immune-related toxicity and survival in metastatic melanoma patients treated with checkpoint immunotherapy.

During this period, I won 3 university and 5 college prizes for academic excellence. Following graduation, I completed a highly competitive Academic Foundation Programme (AFP) and

ambitious project which could transform long-term management for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. At this pivotal stage in my training, taking an extended period away from clinical work will allow me to concentrate fully on my growth as a researcher. I am confident that I will be able to maximise this opportunity, due to my robust training plan, my expert supervisory team, and the dynamic research environment at KCL. Completing this fellowship will position me well for a Clinical Lectureship, and bring me one step closer to my goal of becoming an impactful Clinician Scientist. Have you already No registered for a PhD? Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body? No Contextual Factors Please use this question to detail any contextual factors you wish to make the Selection Committee aware of. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:  Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.  Reduced time spent undertaking research due to a disability or caring responsibilities. This could include any physical or mental difficulty that may have impacted your research career.

Proposed training and development programme I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an academic dermatologist. Systematic Reviews I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a 5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020. I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL), which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R. In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group. Statistics and Computer Programming I have developed a strong practical grounding in statistics over the past 6 years, with experience in summarising
