evidence.text: In addition, I will build on my skills in public communication by attending a 1-day Public Engagement course (Science Museum). Our group has well-established links with the Psoriasis Association and a local inflammatory skin disease PPI network. I will benefit from this extensive expertise in involving patient partners in research. Leadership In my first year, I will complete a 6-day Emerging Clinical Leaders course (The King’s Fund). This will provide valuable leadership training which will maximise the success of this project. In my third year, I will attend a 3-day Future Leaders Academy (European Society for Dermatological Research), focusing on delegation, conflict resolution, and influencing strategic change. This presents a unique opportunity to connect with future leaders in dermatology and receive guidance from experienced mentors, greatly benefitting my postdoctoral career. Research Environment My supervisors have considerable experience supporting PhD students to completion and will provide expertise on key methodological elements of this proposal. I will be hosted by the St John’s Institute of Dermatology, a world- renowned centre of excellence in skin research with an outstanding track record of supporting clinical academic PhD fellows. The KCL Doctoral Development Programme offers a comprehensive suite of training opportunities,

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

Specialty Registrar in 08/2022 - HEE Thames Valley 0 Dermatology NIHR Academic Clinical 08/2019 07/2022 HEE Thames Valley 25 Fellow in Dermatology Academic Foundation Year 08/2018 07/2019 HEE Thames Valley 33

an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) in Dermatology. I have a proven track-record of successfully balancing research with clinical training, having authored 14 peer-reviewed publications with 8 as first author (h- index 9). I am well-equipped for this DRF due to my extensive research experience and strong organisational skills. Research Experience During my AFP, I extracted data and assessed the risk of bias for two systematic reviews for the Cochrane Tobacco Addiction Group, following a 4-day Training Programme organised by Cochrane UK. I also worked with Professor Laura Coates, an academic rheumatologist, to design and run a multicentre study comparing different patient-reported outcome measures in psoriatic arthritis. During this, I completed Good Clinical Practice training, secured ethical approval, wrote the study documents, coordinated patient recruitment and analysed the data. I presented my findings at the 2019 British Psoriatic Arthritis Consortium, winning the runner-up abstract award, with two subsequent first author publications in The Journal of Rheumatology. In parallel, I worked on a multicentre cohort study examining the association between immune-related toxicity and survival in metastatic melanoma patients treated with checkpoint immunotherapy.

Supervised by Professor Benjamin Fairfax, an academic oncologist, I led the project from conception to completion and became proficient in using R for descriptive statistics and survival analysis. My work resulted in a joint first author publication in the British Journal of Cancer. Separately, I characterised the cutaneous toxicities in this cohort, which led to an oral presentation at the 2020 Annual Meeting of the British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) and a first author publication in Clinical and Experimental Dermatology. As part of my subsequent ACF in Dermatology, I completed a PGDip in Health Research with Distinction at the University of Oxford. This provided in-depth training on study design, statistics, diagnostic and screening studies, systematic reviews, and statistical computing using R and Stata. For my 9-month research rotation, I secured £25,000 from Cancer Research UK. This funded single-cell RNA sequencing experiments to investigate gene expression changes in melanoma patients who develop immune-related toxicities following checkpoint immunotherapy. In addition to in-house training on peripheral mononuclear cell extraction, immune cell subset isolation and single-cell sequencing, I attended courses on Linux for Genomics, RNA-seq data analysis and R to

complement my self-directed learning. My work contributed to a co-author publication in Nature Medicine and I won two sought-after travel scholarships to present my research (European Congress of Rheumatology, World Congress of Dermatology). Since completing my ACF, I have immersed myself in full-time dermatology training while actively contributing to research. I am a peer reviewer for the journals Skin Health and Disease and the British Journal of Dermatology and have recently been awarded a prestigious part-time fellowship from the UK Dermatology Clinical Trials Network (receiving the joint-highest score), where I will receive further training in patient-focused clinical research. My diverse research experiences to date have helped me realise that my true passion lies in clinical research with a direct impact on patient care. Being halfway through specialist training, I have experienced many different subspecialties and identified inflammatory skin disease as an area combining my academic and clinical interests. I became a trainee representative for the British Association of Dermatologists Biologics and Immunomodulators Register (BADBIR), a national registry for people with psoriasis on systemic therapy. In addition, I have also recently joined the BAD Biologic Therapy for Psoriasis Guideline Development Group as a trainee representative.

criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

criteria.pros: Strong publication record (14 papers, 8 first author, h-index 9), multiple awards/prizes, and a £25k grant indicate impactful outputs and recognition.
criteria.drawbacks: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.
criteria.rationale: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary covers all required components (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), is well-structured and coherent, uses simple language with minimal unexplained jargon, and strongly emphasizes patient and societal relevance. evidence.text: experience. Methods First, I will review existing research to see how common it is for people to keep their psoriasis under control on tablet or injection treatments. I will also look for individual factors, such as age and weight, which could make this more or less likely. Using two large datasets from the UK and Europe, I will then build computer models to predict how likely it is for someone to keep their psoriasis under control. This prediction will be based on routinely collected clinical data, such as age, ethnicity and smoking history. I will also look at whether including genetic data (i. e. information stored in DNA) improves how well the models perform. Finally, I will host separate group discussions with healthcare professionals (including doctors and nurses) and people with psoriasis. They will provide input on which models would be best suited for use as a tool. They will also help to make the tool more user-friendly. After creating the tool, I will invite healthcare professionals to test it, and further improvements will be made based on their feedback. Patient and Public Involvement The problem this project aims to address has been identified as a research priority by people with psoriasis, their
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text: families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more efficiently. Dissemination I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with patients and the public to develop easily understood summaries which will be circulated widely through charities, newsletters and social media.

evidence.text: Background Psoriasis is a common, incurable inflammatory skin disease associated with high psychosocial morbidity and economic impact. Systemic therapy is often required for individuals with moderate-to-severe psoriasis to achieve disease control (i. e. clear or almost clear skin). Systemic therapy encompasses conventional systemic immunosuppressive therapy, as well as a growing list of newer, highly effective biologics. While the effectiveness of biologics has led to a rapidly expanding cohort who achieve disease control, there still exists no means of predicting whether this will persist longer-term at the individual level. Uncertainty over the longevity of disease control not only causes significant anxiety for people with psoriasis, but also results in the continuation of a one-size-fits-all healthcare model of regular follow-ups regardless of clinical need. This leads to poorer treatment experiences, inefficient resource allocation, and a worsening supply and demand mismatch in the NHS. Aims and Objectives This project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy. To do this, I will:  Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the

1. How is disease control defined in psoriasis? The NICE psoriasis guidelines define disease control as a Physician’s Global Assessment (PGA) of clear/almost clear skin5. This correlates to a Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) ≤2 or a PASI 90 response (i. e. 90% baseline)4, psoriasis5. improvement from where PASI is the gold standard disease severity measure in Achievement of disease control is typically evaluated 2-6 months post-initiation of systemic therapy22. Whether this is sustained long-term (i. e. ≥6 months) is a separate, important issue, which my project will focus on. Loss of disease control (i. e. relapse) is defined as a ≥3 increase in PASI, with a minimum PASI of 523.

2. How feasible is sustained disease control on systemic therapy? The Cochrane living systematic review comparing different systemic therapies only focused on randomised controlled trial (RCT) data ≤6 months post-initiation22. However, open-label extensions (OLE) to RCTs have shed light on the proportion sustaining longer-term disease control. After starting ixekizumab (IL-17 inhibitor), 75% and 73% of individuals exhibited disease control at 3 and 13 months24. In individuals who achieve disease control on risankizumab (IL-23 inhibitor) at 7 months post-initiation, 87% and 81% maintained this at 12 and 24 months25. Comparisons of longer-term RCT data are hindered by heterogeneity of reporting timepoints and trial design. Additionally, the demographics and complexity of trial populations differ from those seen in clinical practice26. To date, only one small-scale North American real-world registry study (n=687) has been published, indicating that a lower proportion of individuals sustain disease control on biologics outside of trial settings (73%/60%/50% at 6/12/18 months respectively)27.

3. What clinical factors are associated with sustained disease control? The rapidly growing cohort achieving initial psoriasis control is a recent phenomenon. As such, researching clinical factors associated with sustained disease control has only become a priority of late, with few studies to date addressing this directly. These include:  A North American real-world registry study (n=687)27, which identified skin itch (visual analogue scale, VAS ≥30/100) or skin pain (VAS ≥45/100) to be associated with increased relapse risk on biologics (HR=1. 47; 95% CI 1. 06-2. 03). Conversely, being biologic-naïve at baseline was associated with reduced risk (HR=0. 78; 95% CI 0. 62-0. 98).  An analysis of guselkumab (IL-23 inhibitor) trial data (n=494)30, which found diabetes (OR=0. 23; 95% CI 0. 05-0. 98) and higher baseline disease impact (OR=0. 94; 95% CI 0.

Methods and Timelines Phase 1 (Months 0-9): Conduct a systematic review to determine the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time, and the clinical and genetic factors associated with this. Phase 2 (Months 7-24): Develop and validate predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using two large-scale real-world datasets from the UK and Europe to ensure generalisability. I will also explore whether including genetic data improves model performance. Phase 3 (Months 19-36): Refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool. Patient and clinician focus groups will determine clinically acceptable levels of model performance, influence the definition of risk categories, and guide user interface design. The tool will be beta tested via a clinician workshop. PPI feedback has been integral to developing this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout, including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.

Research questions  5. 1a – What proportion of individuals sustain psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time?  5. 1b – What clinical/genetic factors are associated with sustained psoriasis control on systemic therapy? A comprehensive search for primary studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO. 2. Phase 2: Model development (7-24 months) Aim Develop predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using real-world data. Research questions  5. 2a – What proportion of individuals sustain disease control over time in real-world settings?  5. 2b – How well can we predict the likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy? Datasets In preparation for this fellowship, I have secured access to two large-scale real-world datasets. BADBIR is a UK and Ireland prospective observational cohort study of people with psoriasis who have commenced systemic therapy within the previous 6 months49. Participants are recruited from >160 hospitals, with >18,000 people registered and >110,000 follow-ups to date. Comprehensive sociodemographic, clinical, treatment and disease severity data are collected at 6-monthly intervals for 3 years, then annually thereafter. Within BADBIR, >6,000 individuals have been genetically profiled via the Biomarkers and Stratification To Optimise outcomes in Psoriasis study (BSTOP), led by my supervisor Professor Smith50. The detailed phenotyping makes the BADBIR data ideal as a discovery and internal validation dataset for exploring RQ5.

6 months, controlling for length of time on treatment. A random effect will be included as individuals will contribute repeated measures of disease control at 6-monthly intervals. Missing data will be dealt with as in RQ5. 2a. Penalisation will use elastic net and lasso approaches to select the minimum subset of variables. I will also explore whether additional machine learning approaches (e. g. support vector machine, random forest, extreme gradient boosting) will improve model performance further. Methods including nested cross validation will be used for internal validation and to prevent overfitting. The HPOS dataset will be used for external validation. Model performance will be assessed using measures of discrimination (C-statistic) and calibration (calibration slope, calibration-in-the-large). Approach B Using all clinical variables within the BADBIR dataset, I will fit, internally validate, and assess model performance using the methods outlined above. This analysis will also be repeated, stratifying by drug class, to evaluate model stability across different treatments. evidence.text: They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact. Dissemination My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social media platforms to maximise public engagement.

2. Outputs and impact Better risk stratification could yield significant benefits across stakeholder groups. Patients Reduce anxiety and empower individuals to change modifiable risk factors, improving satisfaction and long-term outcomes. Clinicians Facilitate earlier intervention for patients at increased risk of relapse, and provide more autonomy and flexibility for those at lower risk. Researchers Inform participant selection for future trials, e. g. those exploring dose minimisation strategies for cohorts likely to sustain disease control. Commissioners Contribute to more efficient service design, e. g. by identifying those most suitable for remote monitoring or PIFU pathways, so resources can be prioritised for individuals with the highest need.

3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will
attend separate online focus groups for open discussion on RQ5. 3a-c. These will be 1.

PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of views, backgrounds and experiences. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options. PPI collaborators will:  Provide input on variable selection for model development in Phase 2.  Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.  Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.  Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the

public. Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to co-present at events. PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualitative data. I have budgeted for PPI, including participant remuneration and expenses, in line with NIHR guidance57. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI activities61. If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the public in your proposed research, please justify why

evidence.text: experience. Methods First, I will review existing research to see how common it is for people to keep their psoriasis under control on tablet or injection treatments. I will also look for individual factors, such as age and weight, which could make this more or less likely. Using two large datasets from the UK and Europe, I will then build computer models to predict how likely it is for someone to keep their psoriasis under control. This prediction will be based on routinely collected clinical data, such as age, ethnicity and smoking history. I will also look at whether including genetic data (i. e. information stored in DNA) improves how well the models perform. Finally, I will host separate group discussions with healthcare professionals (including doctors and nurses) and people with psoriasis. They will provide input on which models would be best suited for use as a tool. They will also help to make the tool more user-friendly. After creating the tool, I will invite healthcare professionals to test it, and further improvements will be made based on their feedback. Patient and Public Involvement The problem this project aims to address has been identified as a research priority by people with psoriasis, their
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text: families and friends, and the healthcare professionals who treat them. The design of the project incorporates further feedback from a meeting I had with people with psoriasis. For example, they emphasised that involving patients and healthcare professionals from the start is essential for developing a useful tool. Patients and the public will continue to be involved during this project through 6-monthly meetings. I will invite them to help plan the group discussions and analyse the conversations after the sessions. They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact This work will help people better understand how likely they are to keep their psoriasis under control while receiving a tablet or injection treatment. It will also allow healthcare professionals to provide better care matched to individual needs. This will improve the patient experience and allow healthcare resources to be used more efficiently. Dissemination I will share my findings by presenting at conferences and by publishing in scientific journals. I will also work with patients and the public to develop easily understood summaries which will be circulated widely through charities, newsletters and social media.

evidence.text: Background Psoriasis is a common, incurable inflammatory skin disease associated with high psychosocial morbidity and economic impact. Systemic therapy is often required for individuals with moderate-to-severe psoriasis to achieve disease control (i. e. clear or almost clear skin). Systemic therapy encompasses conventional systemic immunosuppressive therapy, as well as a growing list of newer, highly effective biologics. While the effectiveness of biologics has led to a rapidly expanding cohort who achieve disease control, there still exists no means of predicting whether this will persist longer-term at the individual level. Uncertainty over the longevity of disease control not only causes significant anxiety for people with psoriasis, but also results in the continuation of a one-size-fits-all healthcare model of regular follow-ups regardless of clinical need. This leads to poorer treatment experiences, inefficient resource allocation, and a worsening supply and demand mismatch in the NHS. Aims and Objectives This project aims to develop a risk stratification tool to predict the likelihood of sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy. To do this, I will:  Evaluate published evidence on the proportion of individuals sustaining disease control over time and the

1. How is disease control defined in psoriasis? The NICE psoriasis guidelines define disease control as a Physician’s Global Assessment (PGA) of clear/almost clear skin5. This correlates to a Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) ≤2 or a PASI 90 response (i. e. 90% baseline)4, psoriasis5. improvement from where PASI is the gold standard disease severity measure in Achievement of disease control is typically evaluated 2-6 months post-initiation of systemic therapy22. Whether this is sustained long-term (i. e. ≥6 months) is a separate, important issue, which my project will focus on. Loss of disease control (i. e. relapse) is defined as a ≥3 increase in PASI, with a minimum PASI of 523.

2. How feasible is sustained disease control on systemic therapy? The Cochrane living systematic review comparing different systemic therapies only focused on randomised controlled trial (RCT) data ≤6 months post-initiation22. However, open-label extensions (OLE) to RCTs have shed light on the proportion sustaining longer-term disease control. After starting ixekizumab (IL-17 inhibitor), 75% and 73% of individuals exhibited disease control at 3 and 13 months24. In individuals who achieve disease control on risankizumab (IL-23 inhibitor) at 7 months post-initiation, 87% and 81% maintained this at 12 and 24 months25. Comparisons of longer-term RCT data are hindered by heterogeneity of reporting timepoints and trial design. Additionally, the demographics and complexity of trial populations differ from those seen in clinical practice26. To date, only one small-scale North American real-world registry study (n=687) has been published, indicating that a lower proportion of individuals sustain disease control on biologics outside of trial settings (73%/60%/50% at 6/12/18 months respectively)27.

3. What clinical factors are associated with sustained disease control? The rapidly growing cohort achieving initial psoriasis control is a recent phenomenon. As such, researching clinical factors associated with sustained disease control has only become a priority of late, with few studies to date addressing this directly. These include:  A North American real-world registry study (n=687)27, which identified skin itch (visual analogue scale, VAS ≥30/100) or skin pain (VAS ≥45/100) to be associated with increased relapse risk on biologics (HR=1. 47; 95% CI 1. 06-2. 03). Conversely, being biologic-naïve at baseline was associated with reduced risk (HR=0. 78; 95% CI 0. 62-0. 98).  An analysis of guselkumab (IL-23 inhibitor) trial data (n=494)30, which found diabetes (OR=0. 23; 95% CI 0. 05-0. 98) and higher baseline disease impact (OR=0. 94; 95% CI 0.

Methods and Timelines Phase 1 (Months 0-9): Conduct a systematic review to determine the proportion of individuals sustaining psoriasis control on systemic therapy over time, and the clinical and genetic factors associated with this. Phase 2 (Months 7-24): Develop and validate predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using two large-scale real-world datasets from the UK and Europe to ensure generalisability. I will also explore whether including genetic data improves model performance. Phase 3 (Months 19-36): Refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool. Patient and clinician focus groups will determine clinically acceptable levels of model performance, influence the definition of risk categories, and guide user interface design. The tool will be beta tested via a clinician workshop. PPI feedback has been integral to developing this proposal. PPI collaborators will be closely involved throughout, including assisting with recruitment strategies, co-facilitating focus groups, and helping with qualitative analysis.

A comprehensive search for primary studies will be performed in Medline, EMBASE, Web of Science and the Cochrane Skin Group Specialised Register. The protocol will be registered on PROSPERO. 2. Phase 2: Model development (7-24 months) Aim Develop predictive models of sustained disease control on systemic therapy using real-world data. Research questions  5. 2a – What proportion of individuals sustain disease control over time in real-world settings?  5. 2b – How well can we predict the likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy? Datasets In preparation for this fellowship, I have secured access to two large-scale real-world datasets. BADBIR is a UK and Ireland prospective observational cohort study of people with psoriasis who have commenced systemic therapy within the previous 6 months49. Participants are recruited from >160 hospitals, with >18,000 people registered and >110,000 follow-ups to date. Comprehensive sociodemographic, clinical, treatment and disease severity data are collected at 6-monthly intervals for 3 years, then annually thereafter. Within BADBIR, >6,000 individuals have been genetically profiled via the Biomarkers and Stratification To Optimise outcomes in Psoriasis study (BSTOP), led by my supervisor Professor Smith50. The detailed phenotyping makes the BADBIR data ideal as a discovery and internal validation dataset for exploring RQ5.

6 months, controlling for length of time on treatment. A random effect will be included as individuals will contribute repeated measures of disease control at 6-monthly intervals. Missing data will be dealt with as in RQ5. 2a. Penalisation will use elastic net and lasso approaches to select the minimum subset of variables. I will also explore whether additional machine learning approaches (e. g. support vector machine, random forest, extreme gradient boosting) will improve model performance further. Methods including nested cross validation will be used for internal validation and to prevent overfitting. The HPOS dataset will be used for external validation. Model performance will be assessed using measures of discrimination (C-statistic) and calibration (calibration slope, calibration-in-the-large). Approach B Using all clinical variables within the BADBIR dataset, I will fit, internally validate, and assess model performance using the methods outlined above. This analysis will also be repeated, stratifying by drug class, to evaluate model stability across different treatments. evidence.text: They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact. Dissemination My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social media platforms to maximise public engagement.

2. Outputs and impact Better risk stratification could yield significant benefits across stakeholder groups. Patients Reduce anxiety and empower individuals to change modifiable risk factors, improving satisfaction and long-term outcomes. Clinicians Facilitate earlier intervention for patients at increased risk of relapse, and provide more autonomy and flexibility for those at lower risk. Researchers Inform participant selection for future trials, e. g. those exploring dose minimisation strategies for cohorts likely to sustain disease control. Commissioners Contribute to more efficient service design, e. g. by identifying those most suitable for remote monitoring or PIFU pathways, so resources can be prioritised for individuals with the highest need.

3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will
attend separate online focus groups for open discussion on RQ5. 3a-c. These will be 1.

PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of views, backgrounds and experiences. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options. PPI collaborators will:  Provide input on variable selection for model development in Phase 2.  Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.  Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.  Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the

public. Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to co-present at events. PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualitative data. I have budgeted for PPI, including participant remuneration and expenses, in line with NIHR guidance57. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI activities61. evidence.text: Proposed training and development programme I have designed the following bespoke training and development plan with the aid of my supervisors. This will ensure I acquire the skills necessary to successfully deliver my proposal, and support my development as an academic dermatologist. Systematic Reviews I have experience with data extraction and risk of bias assessments through co-authoring two Cochrane systematic reviews. Additionally, I completed a 4-day Cochrane Review Author Training Course in 2018 and a 5-day Systematic Reviews course (Oxford University) in 2020. I will build upon these foundations by attending a 4-day Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis of Health Research course (London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine) in my first year, which will provide a refresher of systematic review methodology. This will be followed by a 3-day Introduction to Meta-analysis course (UCL), which will provide a practical toolkit for meta-analysis and heterogeneity assessment using R. In addition to formal courses, I will also be supported via our group’s collaboration with the Cochrane Skin Group. Statistics and Computer Programming I have developed a strong practical grounding in statistics over the past 6 years, with experience in summarising
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: data using descriptive statistics and conducting survival analyses (see research background). I am also familiar with the RStudio environment and have significant prior experience in using R for data wrangling, visualisation and statistical tests. This has prepared me well for this fellowship, as R will be the main programming language used for data analysis. I completed a 5-day Statistical Computing with R and Stata course in 2023 (Oxford University), which provided an introduction to statistical principles relevant to this project, including dealing with missing data, power calculations, regression modelling, and cross validation. While this gave me a basic understanding of different statistical techniques, I would benefit from further formal training during this fellowship. I will attend a 5-day course on Practical Statistics for Epidemiology using R (Oxford University) in my first year to gain experience in interpreting and presenting statistical results, and learn techniques to address bias and confounding. I will also complete a 2-day Regressions with R course (UCL) to learn how to fit different regression models, including logistic regression which is central to my analyses. To prepare for the predictive modelling component of my proposal, I will attend a 3-day Statistical Methods for Risk Prediction and Prognostic
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: Models course (Birmingham University). This will improve my knowledge of statistical methods for developing and validating predictive models. Additionally, I will receive dedicated hands-on training from Dr Niamh Dooley, a statistician at KCL, and Dr Sam Norton, who will supervise the statistical components of my project. Genetic Epidemiology In Phase 2, I will explore whether the predictive performance of clinical models can be meaningfully improved by incorporating genetic data. To better understand different genetic data types and the construction and use of polygenic risk scores, I will attend a 5-day Genetic Epidemiology course (Bristol University). I will also benefit from a collaboration with Professor Michael Simpson at the Department of Genetics in KCL, who has significant expertise in this area. Qualitative Research Methods During my third year, I will conduct focus groups to collate stakeholder perspectives on the models developed. In preparation, I will complete two courses in my second year: a 1-day Introduction to doing Qualitative Interviews course followed by a 5-day Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods course (both Oxford University). These will yield vital knowledge on how to successfully conduct focus groups, and give me practice with coding
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: published by NIHR to support her clinical academic training and I confirm that KCL is committed to these principles and obligations. The St John’s Institute of Dermatology (School of Basic and Medical Biosciences, KCL) is an internationally renowned centre for the delivery of innovative clinical care for patients with skin diseases, cutting-edge education, training, and research. A recent independent RAND assessment ranked St John’s as the leading clinical and research institution for dermatology in the UK. St John’s occupies 1,200 square metres of state-of-the-art laboratory space (Guy’s Hospital campus) and is fully integrated into the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine's School of Basic & Medical Biosciences. An active programme of graduate education is pursued including research training (PhD and MD) and a world-renowned taught course (MSc) in Clinical Dermatology. The Institute excels in research training, with a >95% success record of its Integrated Academic Training Pathway Fellows obtaining external funding from MRC/NIHR. In addition, St John's Institute of Dermatology plays a leading role in the training of dermatologists, both in the UK and around the world.
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: Research at St John’s targets 4 key areas: inflammatory and autoimmune skin disorders (the key area of this research proposal), cutaneous oncology, genetic skin disorders, and skin-environment interactions. Our research is supported by grants from a range of sources including NIHR, MRC, The Wellcome Trust, EU, US Foundations, British Skin Foundation, national charities, and commercial sources. Our department research income averages >£3m per year with a School average research grant income of >£25m per year. The latest submission to the REF confirmed Clinical Medicine was independently rated to have 90% of its research as world leading or internationally excellent (3 or 4*) and 84 % of impact as world leading (4*). King’s College London is part of King’s Health Partners, one of the UK’s five Academic Health Sciences Centres, where world-class research, teaching and clinical practice are brought together for the benefit of patients. The three NHS partners; Guy’s and St Thomas’, King’s College Hospital (KCH) and South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trusts, are among the highest rated Trusts in the country and compete on the world
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: Each supervisor has explicit roles and responsibilities, with expertise directly aligned to the project (e.g., Dr Mahil in psoriasis, Dr Norton in statistics). A structured supervision framework includes weekly/fortnightly meetings, a progression committee, and a Patient and Lay Advisory Group, providing comprehensive mentorship and accountability.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; all signals strongly supported.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; all signals strongly supported.

evidence.text: 1. Fellowship oversight I will lead this project with support from my supervisors who will provide academic, clinical and methodological oversight. We will meet regularly to ensure my research and training progresses according to the Gantt chart. My progress will also be monitored at King’s College London via 6-monthly meetings with an independent Progression Committee.

including courses on effective communication, teaching methods, and career management. Furthermore, the frequent seminars and institute-wide events foster a vibrant environment conducive to collaboration and creativity. Collectively, these factors make KCL an ideal home for this project and a nurturing space for my growth as an academic dermatologist. RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1 Name Dr Satveer Mahil Institution St John’s Institute of Dermatology, King’s College London (KCL) Position Consultant Dermatologist and Reader Description of support provided Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at KCL. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC- funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. Dr Mahil’s translational research focuses on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies for inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She has a strong interest in optimising the management of individuals with psoriasis who have sustained disease control.
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: 3 PhD students (all ongoing), including 1 NIHR DRF. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. I have established an excellent working relationship with Dr Mahil throughout the development of this fellowship proposal and she will be my primary supervisor. We will meet weekly to discuss progress, address unforeseen challenges/barriers, and ensure my training needs are met. RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2 Name Dr Sam Norton Institution King’s College London (KCL) Position Reader in Research Methods and Statistics Description of support provided Dr Sam Norton is a Reader in Research Methods and Statistics at KCL. He has significant expertise in meta- analysis, longitudinal data analysis, and working with large-scale observational cohort studies of chronic disease. He is a KCL Clinical Trials Unit affiliated senior statistician and the capacity development lead for the NIHR Research Support Service delivered by KCL and its partners. He was previously the statistical lead for the NIHR
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text: 1 NIHR ACF, and 1 completed NIHR DRF. We will aim to meet fortnightly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met. The St John’s Institute of Dermatology (School of Basic and Medical Biosciences, KCL) is an internationally renowned centre for the delivery of innovative clinical care for patients with skin diseases, cutting-edge education, training, and research. A recent independent RAND assessment ranked St John’s as the leading clinical and research institution for dermatology in the UK. St John’s occupies 1,200 square metres of state-of-the-art laboratory space (Guy’s Hospital campus) and is fully integrated into the Faculty of Life Sciences & Medicine's School of Basic & Medical Biosciences. An active programme of graduate education is pursued including research training (PhD and MD) and a world-renowned taught course (MSc) in Clinical Dermatology. The Institute excels in research training, with a >95% success record of its Integrated Academic Training Pathway Fellows obtaining external funding from MRC/NIHR. In addition, St John's Institute of Dermatology plays a leading role in the training of dermatologists, both in the UK and around the world.

RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1 Name Dr Satveer Mahil Institution St John’s Institute of Dermatology, King’s College London (KCL) Position Consultant Dermatologist and Reader Description of support provided Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at KCL. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC- funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. Dr Mahil’s translational research focuses on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies for inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She has a strong interest in optimising the management of individuals with psoriasis who have sustained disease control.

evidence.text: These experiences have highlighted critical unmet needs in psoriasis research, and have provided inspiration for this DRF proposal, which has been subsequently refined through discussions with supervisors and PPI collaborators. Teaching Experience I have taught medical students in classroom, ward and outpatient settings. I also designed and implemented a well-received junior doctor-led teaching programme for medical students during my AFP. Additionally, I have completed a Developing Teaching and Learning for Medical Educators course, and have supervised junior colleagues for multiple audits and quality improvement projects. Leadership and Management Experience During my AFP, I sat on the Steering Committee of a national conference for junior doctors and medical students to showcase their research. Within my department, I chaired weekly clinicopathological correlation meetings over a 6-month period, and I am currently responsible for organising monthly regional complex cases meetings. Public Engagement Experience I recently organised a PPI advisory event, collecting valuable feedback on my project proposal. I hope to utilise this fellowship to build further experience in public engagement and involvement, supported by the extensive expertise within our group. Fellowship Impact This fellowship provides the perfect platform to advance my career as an academic dermatologist, through an

3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will
attend separate online focus groups for open discussion on RQ5. 3a-c. These will be 1.

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this proposal I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13 involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified. My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised. Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age, ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and systemic therapies for psoriasis. All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they emphasised that:  A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people on systemic therapy.  Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.  Personalised follow-up strategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket approach. Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the

likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which have shaped my proposal:  Disease control maintained over the longer term, i. e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1 and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.  Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will compare models including and excluding genetic data.  To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user interface design and beta testing. Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpful feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary. Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in the proposed research, including any training and support provided

PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of views, backgrounds and experiences. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options. PPI collaborators will:  Provide input on variable selection for model development in Phase 2.  Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.  Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.  Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the

evidence.text: They will also ensure the research is inclusive and relevant to diverse communities. Impact Successful delivery of the risk stratification tool could reduce patient anxiety, empower individuals to address modifiable risk factors, and personalise long-term management strategies. For example, individuals likely to sustain disease control will benefit from increased autonomy and flexibility and may be suited to remote monitoring or patient-initiated follow-up pathways. Higher-risk individuals may be appropriate for adjunct therapies with closer monitoring, facilitating earlier intervention for flares to reduce disease impact. Dissemination My findings will be disseminated widely through high-impact peer-reviewed publications and presentations at national and international conferences. I will co-develop Plain English Summaries and infographics with my PPI collaborators, which will be shared via UK and European psoriasis patient associations, newsletters, and social media platforms to maximise public engagement.

My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised. Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age, ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. evidence.text: 3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will

representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with disabilities. All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped into potential themes using an iterative process59. I will involve two PPI collaborators in the data analysis to minimise bias. These focus groups will establish stakeholder perspectives on required parameters for a clinically acceptable risk stratification tool. These perspectives are critical for maximising the clinical utility of risk stratification in practice and will provide generalisable insights beyond this project. Beta testing Informed by focus group discussions, I will refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool, taking the form of an online interactive dashboard (built using R Shiny). This will be trialled in a 2-hour beta testing workshop involving 20 multidisciplinary healthcare professionals. This sample size is pragmatic, based on recruitment feasibility, and will provide sufficient data to determine tool usability for future work. The primary outcome will be the proportion who successfully input data to obtain an output, and the secondary outcome will be the data input

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this proposal I originally developed this proposal with the findings of the 2018 James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership13 involving patients, carers and clinicians in mind. This highlighted research into factors affecting sustained disease control in psoriasis as a key unmet need, aligning with 3 of the top 10 priorities identified. My proposal has since been refined further based on feedback from a PPI advisory group meeting I organised. Participants were recruited from local networks and the Psoriasis Association, with diversity in terms of age, ethnicity, geographical location and disease duration. They also had varied experiences of different topical and systemic therapies for psoriasis. All participants strongly supported my research question and felt that it was relevant. In particular, they emphasised that:  A better understanding of factors affecting sustained psoriasis control would reduce uncertainty for people on systemic therapy.  Identification of modifiable risk factors may empower individuals to make appropriate lifestyle changes.  Personalised follow-up strategies co-designed by patients and clinicians are preferable to a blanket approach. Participants were enthusiastic about the idea of a risk stratification tool providing individualised estimates of the

likelihood of sustained disease control on systemic therapy. The group highlighted the following key points, which have shaped my proposal:  Disease control maintained over the longer term, i. e. ≥6 months, is especially important. Hence, Phases 1 and 2 focus on sustaining disease control for ≥6 months as a key outcome.  Not everyone will be comfortable with genetic data being used. However, the group agreed that there is value in exploring whether genetic data can improve model performance. Consequently, Phase 2 will compare models including and excluding genetic data.  To maximise the tool’s clinical utility, early involvement of patients and clinicians is essential. This influenced Phase 3 of my proposal, in which stakeholder engagement is central to model refinement, user interface design and beta testing. Following this event, 4 individuals expressed an interest in continued involvement in the project as part of a Patient and Lay Advisory Group. They have also provided helpful feedback for the proposal’s Plain English Summary. PPI is integral to the success of my research, particularly given its potential to provide more autonomy and flexibility for individuals likely to sustain disease control long-term. To this end, I will establish a Patient and Lay Advisory Group of 6 individuals, including people with psoriasis and their carers/family members. Individuals will be recruited from my initial PPI session, the Psoriasis Association and the well-established inflammatory skin disease PPI network led by my supervisors at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology. This will ensure a diversity of views, backgrounds and experiences. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings around key decision points. Based on feedback, meetings will be virtual to minimise disruption to daily schedules and maximise inclusivity. To reduce the potential for digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and provide phone-in options. PPI collaborators will:  Provide input on variable selection for model development in Phase 2.  Inform recruitment strategies for Phase 3, especially for underserved groups.  Help design a topic guide and co-facilitate the focus groups in Phase 3.  Assist with interpreting research findings and opine on their relevance to people with psoriasis and the

public. Additionally, they will review communications on research findings intended for patients and the public. Guided by the NIHR equality, diversity and inclusion toolkit, these will be widely disseminated via charities, established European patient networks and partners, newsletters and social media. I will also leverage the annual national PPI webinars co-organised by the Psoriasis Association and St John’s DermAcademy (>100 attendees per event) to share my results. Where appropriate, I will invite PPI collaborators to co-author peer-reviewed publications and to co-present at events. PPI collaborators will be invited to attend PPI training sessions run by Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Trust and by the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. For those involved in Phase 3, I will provide additional training on conducting focus groups and interpreting qualitative data. I have budgeted for PPI, including participant remuneration and expenses, in line with NIHR guidance57. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI activities61. If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the public in your proposed research, please justify why

criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusion is planned across the entire research lifecycle: variable selection, recruitment, co-facilitation of focus groups, data interpretation, and dissemination; accessibility addressed (virtual meetings, phone-in, digital inclusion).
criteria.drawbacks: No explicit plan for ongoing evaluation or adjustment of inclusion strategies over the project period; while feedback loops exist, systematic monitoring is not documented.
criteria.rationale: No explicit plan for ongoing evaluation or adjustment of inclusion strategies over the project period; while feedback loops exist, systematic monitoring is not documented.

evidence.text: 3. Phase 3: Developing the risk stratification tool (19-36 months) Aim Refine Phase 2 predictive models into a risk stratification tool, utilising a co-design approach involving patients and clinicians to enhance acceptability for end-users. Research questions  5. 3a – Which models are clinically acceptable for development into a risk stratification tool?  5. 3b – What is the best way to stratify a continuous risk spectrum into discrete risk groups?  5. 3c – What barriers and enablers affect the use of a risk stratification tool, and how acceptable is it to use such a tool to guide psoriasis management? Design Phase 2 will produce a range of models designed to predict sustained disease control on systemic therapy, differing in their complexity, accuracy, and included variables. In Phase 3, I will use focus groups to assess stakeholder views on these models to inform subsequent tool development. The practicality of using the tool will be evaluated in a beta testing workshop. All participants will be reimbursed57. Focus groups People with psoriasis and multidisciplinary healthcare professionals (dermatologists/pharmacists/nurses) will

representation from underserved groups, including lower socioeconomic classes, ethnic minorities and those with disabilities. All discussions will be audio-recorded and transcribed verbatim to enable inductive thematic analysis using NVivo software. A systematic approach will be used to organise collected data into relevant codes, which will be grouped into potential themes using an iterative process59. I will involve two PPI collaborators in the data analysis to minimise bias. These focus groups will establish stakeholder perspectives on required parameters for a clinically acceptable risk stratification tool. These perspectives are critical for maximising the clinical utility of risk stratification in practice and will provide generalisable insights beyond this project. Beta testing Informed by focus group discussions, I will refine the predictive models into a risk stratification tool, taking the form of an online interactive dashboard (built using R Shiny). This will be trialled in a 2-hour beta testing workshop involving 20 multidisciplinary healthcare professionals. This sample size is pragmatic, based on recruitment feasibility, and will provide sufficient data to determine tool usability for future work. The primary outcome will be the proportion who successfully input data to obtain an output, and the secondary outcome will be the data input

findings.implication: Applicant CV: Degrees and clinical/academic positions demonstrate relevant research experience and formal training.
Applicant CV: Research grants, multiple publications, and numerous prizes/awards indicate impactful research outputs and recognitions.
Applicant CV: Lead applicant on a research grant and multiple awards suggest trajectory towards research leadership.
Applicant CV: Clinical academic roles (Academic Clinical Fellow, Academic Foundation Year) demonstrate commitment to practitioner-academic career.
Applicant Research Background: Applicant articulates clear career vision (academic dermatologist) and positions DRF as next step, with strong mentorship and institutional support.
Applicant Research Background: Section explains why this award is the right step, how it builds on prior experience, and develops partnerships via BADBIR and guideline group.
Applicant Research Background: Extensive research experience across rheumatology, oncology, and dermatology with relevant techniques (single-cell seq, survival analysis) and formal training.
Applicant Research Background: Strong publication record (14 papers, 8 first author, h-index 9), prizes, and £25k grant indicate impactful outputs.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates leadership by leading projects, supervising juniors, chairing meetings, and sitting on national committee.
Applicant Research Background: Explicit commitment to practitioner-academic career through AFP, ACF, and stated goal of becoming academic dermatologist.
Applicant Research Background: Formal training includes PGDip in Health Research with Distinction, Good Clinical Practice, and specialised courses (Linux, RNA-seq).
Applicant Research Background: Acquired targeted skills (R, Stata, single-cell sequencing) directly applicable to proposed psoriasis research.
Applicant Research Background: Training positioned as stepping stone to Clinical Lectureship and independent Clinician Scientist career.
Applicant Research Background: Organised a PPI advisory event to collect feedback on project proposal, showing early integration of lived experience.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract identifies clear knowledge gap (no means to predict long-term disease control), supporting pr.2.c.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract provides phased timeline and clear feasibility indicators (Months 0-9, 7-24, 19-36), supporting pr.3.b.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract explicitly describes methods (systematic review, predictive modeling, focus groups) aligned with aims, supporting pr.4.a.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract details patient benefits (reduced anxiety, personalized management) and specific outcomes, supporting pr.5.a and pr.5.c.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract describes PPI involvement at multiple stages (co-facilitation, qualitative analysis) and inclusive design, supporting pr.6.a and pr.6.c.
Scientific Abstract: Abstract includes dissemination plan (publications, conferences, infographics via patient associations) linking study to public benefit, supporting wp.3.

Detailed Research Plan: Extensive literature review (sections 3.1-3.5) cites multiple studies, identifies gaps, and justifies need for systematic review, strongly supporting evidence review quality and relevance.
